stewart_read: Jack has just disclosed at 00:13 that Ion already emailed him a quote. Bucky's response — 'Alright. What's going on?' — is a generic prompt that treats this as a normal cold-open. There is no recycled-lead or prior-touch variant in Bucky's toolkit. The correct move here is a reframe that uses the prior quote as a bridge: something like 'Perfect — I actually want to make sure that design is built around your specific situation, not just a generic number. Let me ask you a couple of quick questions.' That reframe converts the prior quote from a redundancy signal into a reason to stay on the call. Bucky doesn't have this move, and the absence of it sets the checklist tone that produces the profanity at 01:22. Strategic-vs-reactive: this is reactive — there is no evidence Bucky is deliberately deferring the reframe to set up a later move. The generic prompt is the only move he has.
alternative_hypothesis: Bucky is deliberately keeping the intro neutral to avoid over-committing before he knows more about the prior quote's content.
coaching_implication: Manager should build a prior-touch intro variant with Bucky: when a customer opens with 'you already sent me something,' the rep's job is to reframe the call as quality-control on that prior quote, not a restart of the process. This is the single most actionable gap on this call — it changes the entire tone of the verify section that follows.

quote: Black Hills. Those are some of the questions on your fucking questionnaire.
stewart_read: Jack's profanity is a direct signal that the checklist execution of the verify section has crossed a threshold. The schema notes that verify 'should sound like a friendly chat' and that 'bottom setters check boxes mechanically' — this is the mechanical failure made audible. The recycled-lead coaching implication is also relevant: if Ion already has data from the prior quote email, Bucky may be re-asking questions that are already on file, which compounds the irritation. Bucky's response ('Do you know what you're spending on a monthly average?') ignores the complaint entirely and continues the checklist — no acknowledgment, no repair, no pivot. Strategic-vs-reactive: reactive. There is no evidence of a deliberate choice to absorb the complaint and continue; Bucky simply doesn't have a repair move.
alternative_hypothesis: Bucky is choosing to stay on task rather than engage with the profanity, treating it as venting rather than a signal requiring response.
coaching_implication: Manager should work with Bucky on a one-line repair for this moment: something like 'Fair point — let me skip what you've already covered and just fill in the gaps.' This acknowledges the frustration, validates the customer's time, and reframes the remaining questions as additive rather than redundant. The repair doesn't need to be elaborate — it needs to exist.

quote: I don't want it mounted on my roof. I got five acres. I like it mounted on the ground.
stewart_read: Jack's disclosure of five acres is an enthusiasm signal that Bucky walks past entirely. A customer with five acres who prefers ground-mount is not a low-engagement lead — he has thought about the installation, has a specific preference, and is describing a property with meaningful solar potential. The ground-mount preference is a constraint, but it is also evidence of genuine interest and planning. Bucky's response treats only the constraint and ignores the signal. Whether Ion can serve this customer depends on ground-mount policy (see coaching_implication), but the enthusiasm embedded in the disclosure — 'I've got the land, I've thought about this' — deserved acknowledgment before the capability statement. Per schema numeric_discipline: the bill amount ($340/month) and property size suggest potential value, but Stewart cannot quantify without Ion's conversion data. This is a coaching gap on financials.
alternative_hypothesis: Bucky correctly identifies that Ion cannot serve this customer and ends the call efficiently rather than wasting time on a dead lead.
coaching_implication: Two questions for the manager: (1) Does Ion have any ground-mount capability, partner referral, or future-state option that Bucky could have offered? If yes, this is a hard training gap — Bucky didn't know the product. If no, the call end is correct but the delivery is still coachable (see 02:08 entry). (2) Even if Ion can't do ground mounts, the five-acre disclosure and $340 bill are worth acknowledging before closing — a customer this engaged may be worth a referral or a future touch.

quote: Okay. We only do roofs and many like, residential roofs for the home. We can't do ground mounts.
stewart_read: This is the call-ending statement. Bucky delivers a flat capability limitation with no curiosity, no alternative, and no attempt to understand whether the constraint is absolute. The schema's changed_mind rebuttal ('did you get a chance to look at the actual design with the numbers yet?') is partially available here — Jack hasn't seen a full design, only a quote email — but it requires adaptation because Jack has already signaled prior contact at 00:13. The verbatim rebuttal ('most people tell me they're not interested until they see how much they could actually save') is undercut by the fact that Jack has already been quoted; the rep would need to reframe around the ground-mount constraint specifically, not generic interest. A more targeted move: 'I hear you — before I let you go, is the ground-mount a hard requirement, or is it more about keeping the roof clear? Because sometimes there are options worth knowing about.' That question costs 10 seconds and either surfaces flexibility or confirms the DQ cleanly. Bucky doesn't attempt it. Strategic-vs-reactive: reactive. The flat statement is the only move Bucky makes.
alternative_hypothesis: Bucky knows Ion's policy is absolute on ground mounts and is correctly ending a DQ'd call rather than wasting the customer's time.
coaching_implication: Manager should clarify Ion's ground-mount policy with Bucky and establish a one-question probe for constraint-based objections: 'Is that a hard requirement, or is there flexibility?' If the policy is truly absolute, the coaching is on delivery — end the call with warmth, not a flat statement. If there's any flexibility or referral path, this is a product-knowledge gap that cost a qualified lead.

quote: Oh, I didn't I'm not interested.
stewart_read: Jack's 'I'm not interested' is a direct consequence of the flat capability statement at 02:08 — it is not an independent objection but a response to being told Ion can't serve him. The changed_mind rebuttal is technically available ('did you get a chance to look at the actual design with the numbers yet?') but is significantly undercut here: Jack has already received a quote email (00:13), so the 'you haven't seen the numbers' framing doesn't land cleanly. Any curiosity-creation attempt at this point would need to be grounded in the ground-mount constraint specifically — 'before I let you go, is there any scenario where roof-mount works for you?' — rather than the generic rebuttal. Bucky's response ('Thanks. Have a great day.') is a clean exit but represents zero attempt at recovery. Whether recovery was viable is the coaching question.
alternative_hypothesis: Bucky correctly reads Jack's tone as fully closed and ends the call professionally rather than burning goodwill on a dead lead.
coaching_implication: Manager should use this moment to distinguish between 'I'm not interested' as a soft refusal (where curiosity creation applies) and 'I'm not interested' as a consequence of a stated capability gap (where the probe is about the constraint, not general interest). Bucky needs a constraint-probe script: one question that tests whether the DQ is absolute before accepting it.

stewart_read: Jack's response to the average-bill question is a single letter — 'J.' — followed by a 16-second gap before Bucky re-asks the question at 01:47. This is a minor call-management note: the gap suggests either a transcription artifact (Jack may have said something inaudible) or a moment of confusion. Bucky's re-ask at 01:47 is handled cleanly, and Jack answers at 01:54 with $340. The gap itself is not a coaching priority but is worth flagging as a call-management note — if Jack was confused or distracted, a brief acknowledgment ('Sorry, I may have cut out — what was that?') would have been cleaner than a 16-second silence followed by a repeat.
alternative_hypothesis: The 'J.' is a transcription artifact and the gap is a connection issue, not a rep failure.
coaching_implication: Low priority. If the manager reviews the audio and confirms a real gap, coach Bucky on a quick recovery line for moments of confusion. If it's a transcription artifact, disregard.
